1	# Ethereum vs other L1s
2	
3	Source: ChatGPT | Date: 2025-11-18
4	
5	At a very high level, EthereumÔÇÖs main advantages over other L1s like Solana, BNB Chain (BSC), Tron, etc. are:
6	
7	1. deeper security / decentralization,  
8	2. the richest and most battle-tested smart-contract ecosystem,  
9	3. being the canonical ÔÇťbase layerÔÇŁ for a rapidly growing rollup (L2) universe,  
10	4. strong standards and institutional/regulatory positioning.
11	
12	The flip side (which you already know) is that other L1s typically win on raw throughput and UX today.
13	
14	IÔÇÖll go through the main axes.
15	
16	---
17	
18	## 1. Security and decentralization
19	
20	### Validator set size and openness
21	
22	Ethereum has by far the largest validator set in crypto. Even after a recent ~10% drop, there are ~1M active validators, with more ETH queued to enter staking. ţłÇciteţłéturn0search16ţłéturn0search25ţłü  
23	
24	By contrast:
25	
26	- **BNB Chain (BSC)** uses a Proof-of-Staked Authority model with ~45 active validators, of which 21 act as ÔÇťcabinet validatorsÔÇŁ with higher governance weight. ţłÇciteţłéturn2search0ţłéturn2search18ţłü  
27	- **Tron** runs on Delegated PoS with just 27 ÔÇťSuper RepresentativesÔÇŁ producing blocks and handling governance. ţłÇciteţłéturn2search1ţłéturn2search19ţłü  
28	
29	Solana has many more validators than BSC/Tron (in the low thousands), but still orders of magnitude fewer than Ethereum, and the count has been volatile and a focus of ongoing decentralization work. ţłÇciteţłéturn0search5ţłéturn0search26ţłü  
30	
31	This matters because:
32	
33	- A large, geographically and organizationally diverse validator set is harder to capture or censor.
34	- On Ethereum, almost anyone can stake (directly or via liquid staking) with open-source clients; BSC and Tron are structurally permissioned at the validator level.
35	
36	### Economic security
37	
38	The value of assets and stake secured on Ethereum is still the largest by a wide margin. Ethereum alone accounts for more than half of DeFi TVL (Ôëł$50ÔÇô70B depending on date/methodology) and remains the dominant chain by TVL overall. ţłÇciteţłéturn0search6ţłéturn0search10ţłéturn0search23ţłü  
39	
40	Large TVL + large staked value + mature liquid-staking ecosystem (Lido, Rocket Pool, restaking protocols, etc.) create:
41	
42	- Huge economic cost to attack the chain.
43	- A strong incentive for institutions/custodians to standardize on Ethereum for ÔÇťserious moneyÔÇŁ use cases.
44	
45	BNB, Tron, Solana each have material TVL, but theyÔÇÖre still smaller individually and often more concentrated in a handful of apps.
46	
47	---
48	
49	## 2. Ecosystem depth, tooling, and network effects
50	
51	### Developer base and tooling
52	
53	Almost all data sources still show **EVM as the most active tech stack** by total developers, even though Solana has recently led in *new* dev growth. ţłÇciteţłéturn0search7ţłéturn0search21ţłéturn0search24ţłéturn0search15ţłü  
54	
55	Implications:
56	
57	- The **default smart-contract mental model is Ethereum/EVM**. Most infra tools, security tooling, libraries, audit practices, hard-won lessons, etc. are built first for Ethereum and then ported elsewhere.
58	- BSCÔÇÖs main advantage here is *EVM compatibility* plus lower fees, but thatÔÇÖs also why much of BSCÔÇÖs ecosystem is direct EVM forks ÔÇô Ethereum remains the origin.
59	
60	### Token and contract standards
61	
62	ERC-20 and ERC-721 (and now ERC-1155, ERC-4626, etc.) are still the canonical token/NFT/vault standards across crypto, with ERC-20 in particular being the ÔÇťgold standardÔÇŁ for fungible tokens and DeFi primitives. ţłÇciteţłéturn1search1ţłéturn1search7ţłéturn1search17ţłéturn1search19ţłü  
63	
64	Other chainsÔÇÖ standards (BEP-20 on BSC, TRC-20 on Tron, SPL tokens on Solana) are usually:
65	
66	- Copies or adaptations of ERC standards; and
67	- Often designed specifically to interoperate with Ethereum/EVM flows (e.g., bridged ERC-20s).
68	
69	So ERC standards give Ethereum a **coordination and composability advantage**: wallets, exchanges, custody platforms, and dApps treat ÔÇťERC-styleÔÇŁ assets as the default.
70	
71	### Liquidity and composability
72	
73	Because Ethereum is where the earliest and largest DeFi systems grew up, a disproportionate share of:
74	
75	- Deepest liquidity pools (major DEXes, lending protocols, LSDs).
76	- Long-tail assets.
77	- Derivative / structured products.
78	
79	still lives on Ethereum mainnet + its L2s. EthereumÔÇÖs share of total DeFi TVL and protocol count is still the largest globally. ţłÇciteţłéturn0search14ţłéturn0search31ţłéturn0search20ţłü  
80	
81	You can think of it this way:
82	
83	- Solana/BSC/Tron may optimize for single-app UX and throughput.
84	- Ethereum optimizes for **composable capital markets** where assets, collateral, and protocols interlock under a shared security and standards umbrella.
85	
86	---
87	
88	## 3. The rollup (L2) ecosystem: modular vs monolithic
89	
90	This is arguably EthereumÔÇÖs biggest strategic edge now.
91	
92	Ethereum has become the **base settlement layer** for a large and rapidly growing Layer-2 ecosystem (Arbitrum, Optimism, Base, zkSync, Starknet, Scroll, etc.), all of which post state/data to Ethereum for security. ţłÇciteţłéturn1search0ţłéturn1search2ţłéturn1search14ţłü  
93	
94	Key points:
95	
96	- L2s already secure tens of billions in TVL and a large share of DEX volume. ţłÇciteţłéturn1academia20ţłéturn1search8ţłéturn1search6ţłü  
97	- They offer **Solana-like (or better) UX** ÔÇô cheap, fast tx ÔÇô while inheriting EthereumÔÇÖs security and asset base.
98	- Different rollups specialize: DeFi depth (Arbitrum), consumer/retail (Base), Superchain / OP Stack alignment (Optimism), ZK-heavy use cases (Starknet, zkSync).
99	
100	EthereumÔÇÖs advantage vs other monolithic L1s is that its scaling roadmap is:
101	
102	- **Horizontal and competitive**: many L2s experimenting in parallel.
103	- **Security-anchored**: rollups publish data to Ethereum, avoiding extra trust assumptions.
104	
105	Solana, BSC, Tron scale *within* their own L1, but they donÔÇÖt yet have an equivalent, mature, multi-rollup ecosystem using them only as a settlement base.
106	
107	---
108	
109	## 4. Governance, neutrality, and credible long-term positioning
110	
111	### Credible neutrality vs corporate control
112	
113	- Ethereum governance is messy but relatively **decentralized and politically neutral**: multiple clients, multiple client teams, public EIPs, and a community that routinely pushes back on core devs.
114	- BSC/BNB Chain is tightly linked to Binance; it uses a small validator set largely aligned with the ecosystemÔÇÖs core institutions and has been criticized for centralization and the ability to coordinate chain halts. ţłÇciteţłéturn2search18ţłéturn2search22ţłü  
115	- Tron revolves around 27 Super Representatives and is heavily associated with a single founder/organization, which concentrates both technical and political power. ţłÇciteţłéturn2search1ţłéturn2search21ţłéturn2search19ţłü  
116	
117	For many institutions and regulators, EthereumÔÇÖs **ÔÇťpublic good / credibly neutralÔÇŁ narrative** is more palatable than chains closely tied to a single exchange or founder.
118	
119	### Regulatory perception and institutional adoption
120	
121	Ethereum is the reference point for:
122	
123	- Spot and futures ETFs (in multiple jurisdictions).
124	- On-chain RWAs and tokenization frameworks.
125	- Large-scale experiments in CBDCs / wholesale settlement, which often use EVM or Ethereum-compatible testnets.
126	
127	The legal and compliance tooling, custody infrastructure, and risk frameworks around Ethereum are therefore more mature than for most other L1s ÔÇô which reinforces the ÔÇťinstitutional base layerÔÇŁ thesis.
128	
129	---
130	
131	## 5. Concrete comparisons vs Solana, BSC, Tron
132	
133	Very compressed, ignoring a lot of nuance:
134	
135	### Versus Solana
136	
137	SolanaÔÇÖs strengths: very high throughput, single L1 UX, strong new-dev momentum, and surging DeFi/NFT ecosystems. ţłÇciteţłéturn0search21ţłéturn0search27ţłéturn0search17ţłü  
138	
139	EthereumÔÇÖs key edges:
140	
141	- Much larger total economic/security footprint and more conservative design.
142	- Rich L2 ecosystem providing Solana-style UX with Ethereum security.
143	- De facto standards and infra (ERCs, tooling, audits, wallets, custody).
144	
145	### Versus BSC / BNB Chain
146	
147	BSCÔÇÖs strengths: low fees, huge user base via Binance, strong retail/retail-CEX funnel, high daily activity. ţłÇciteţłéturn2search12ţłéturn0search31ţłü  
148	
149	EthereumÔÇÖs edges:
150	
151	- Orders-of-magnitude more validators; BSC remains structurally semi-permissioned with ~45 validators. ţłÇciteţłéturn2search18ţłéturn2search14ţłéturn2search10ţłü  
152	- Stronger decentralization narrative and lower governance capture risk.
153	- Origin of most DeFi/NFT/token standards; BSC largely follows/forks Ethereum.
154	
155	### Versus Tron
156	
157	TronÔÇÖs strengths: very high stablecoin transfer volume and cheap, fast transfers, especially for USDT in emerging markets.
158	
159	EthereumÔÇÖs edges:
160	
161	- Far broader smart-contract and DeFi ecosystem; Tron is still relatively narrow and payments-centric.
162	- Much more decentralized validator set versus TronÔÇÖs 27 Super Representatives.
163	- Rich L2 and RWA/tokenization ecosystems; wider institutional tooling.
164	
165	---
166	
167	## 6. Where Ethereum is weaker
168	
169	For completeness, the main *disadvantages* vs other L1s:
170	
171	- Base-layer throughput and latency: Solana and others are faster and cheaper on L1 today.
172	- User UX on mainnet: gas fees and congestion are still an issue if youÔÇÖre not on an L2.
173	- Single-app performance: Solana-style monolithic design can be superior for highly optimized workloads that want one consistent execution environment without rollup fragmentation.
174	
175	---
176	
177	## Short version
178	
179	EthereumÔÇÖs moat is not ÔÇťcheapest or fastest L1.ÔÇŁ Its advantage is:
180	
181	- Deep security and decentralization.
182	- Being the origin and center of the EVM + ERC standards universe.
183	- The richest, most composable DeFi/RWA/NFT ecosystem and the largest TVL.
184	- Acting as the settlement layer for a competitive field of L2s that inherit its security.
185	
186	Solana, BSC, Tron, etc. are increasingly strong *adjacent* ecosystems, but they still tend to interoperate with EthereumÔÇÖs standards and liquidity rather than replace it as the neutral base layer.

10	
11	To tailor the research effectively, could you clarify a few things:
12	
13	1. Are you primarily interested in institutional or retail investment perspectives?
14	2. Should the focus be on regulatory, technological, or market adoption developments?
15	3. Do you want analysis of specific tokenization projects or platforms built on Ethereum or Solana?

33	Thanks for the clarification. IÔÇÖll explore recent developments over the past 12 months that shape the fundamental investment case for Ethereum and Solana, especially in the context of asset tokenization.

41	# Asset Tokenization on Ethereum vs. Solana (2024ÔÇô2025) ÔÇô A Comprehensive Analysis
42	
43	## Introduction and Overview of Asset Tokenization (2024ÔÇô2025)
44	
45	Asset tokenization ÔÇô the process of creating blockchain-based tokens that represent ownership of real-world assets (RWAs) ÔÇô has emerged as one of the fastest-growing use cases in crypto over the past 12ÔÇô18 months. In 2024 alone, the on-chain tokenized RWA market (excluding stablecoins) nearly doubled, reaching about **$15.2 billion by December 2024**. By mid-2025, that figure surpassed **$24 billion** (excluding stablecoins), marking a **380% increase from 2022 levels**. (Including fiat-backed stablecoins ÔÇô essentially tokenized cash ÔÇô the total tokenized asset market is over $200B, though this analysis will focus on non-cash assets like securities, real estate, and commodities.) Industry projections are optimistic: **10ÔÇô30% of global assets could be tokenized by 2030ÔÇô2034**, bridging trillions of dollars from traditional finance into blockchain networks. This trend represents a fundamental shift, positioning RWAs as a bridge between the ~$400 trillion traditional asset markets and the on-chain economy. 
46	
47	Several factors are driving this tokenization boom. High interest rates in 2024ÔÇô2025 made yield-bearing RWAs (e.g. tokenized **U.S. Treasuries**) especially attractive on-chain, while blockchainÔÇÖs 24/7 markets and fractional ownership broadened access to traditionally illiquid assets like **real estate** and **private credit**. Major institutions have moved beyond pilot projects and are now launching production-scale offerings: for example, **BlackRock, JPMorgan, Franklin Templeton, Apollo Global Management**, and **UBS** all rolled out tokenized asset initiatives in 2024ÔÇô2025. Governments and regulators are also increasingly supportive, seeing tokenization as a way to modernize financial markets. The past year has thus seen convergence of technological maturity, institutional adoption, and regulatory clarity that bolsters the fundamental investment case for blockchain networks enabling asset tokenization. 
48	
49	**Ethereum and Solana** have emerged as two prominent public blockchains hosting tokenized assets, each with distinct strengths. **Ethereum** is currently the dominant RWA platform by value, **hosting ~$7.5B (Ôëł59% market share) across 335 tokenized products as of mid-2025**. It enjoys deep liquidity (over $130B in stablecoins on Ethereum) and a decade-long security track record that inspires institutional confidence. **Solana**, while a later entrant, is a high-performance challenger that gained momentum in 2024ÔÇô2025 ÔÇô its RWA market grew over **200% in the first half of 2025**. Solana now hosts around **$350M in tokenized assets** (Ôëł3% market share) across a smaller number of products, but it offers ultra-fast settlement and negligible fees, appealing to use cases that demand speed and low cost. In the sections below, we provide a structured analysis of Ethereum vs. Solana in the asset tokenization space ÔÇô examining institutional and retail perspectives, regulatory and technological developments, market adoption trends, and key tokenization projects on each network.
50	
51	## Ethereum: The Institutional Standard for Tokenization
52	
53	Ethereum has positioned itself as the **ÔÇťinstitutional standardÔÇŁ** for asset tokenization. As the most mature smart contract platform, it benefits from network effects: a large base of users and developers, hundreds of DeFi applications, and integration into enterprise workflows. **Major financial institutions consistently choose Ethereum** for issuing tokenized assets, leveraging its broad user network and liquidity. For example, in March 2024 **BlackRock launched its inaugural tokenized fund (the BUIDL fund) on Ethereum**, offering exposure to short-term U.S. Treasuries. Within weeks, BUIDL became the worldÔÇÖs largest on-chain U.S. Treasury product ÔÇô reaching about **$657M** AUM in its first six weeks and later growing past **$1ÔÇô2.7B** as more investors poured in. BlackRockÔÇÖs choice of Ethereum underscores the trust in its security and operational stability. As *RedStone* research notes, EthereumÔÇÖs **decade-long track record and deep liquidity (e.g. ~$131B in stablecoins)** give it a ÔÇťdurable moatÔÇŁ for tokenized finance despite newer alternatives.
54	
55	From a **technological perspective**, EthereumÔÇÖs recent and upcoming upgrades aim to support its growing RWA ecosystem. The move to Proof-of-Stake in 2022 (the Merge) improved energy efficiency and set the stage for scalability upgrades. In late 2023, the network implemented proto-danksharding (EIP-4844, part of the ÔÇťSurgeÔÇŁ roadmap) to eventually enable **lower fees via Layer-2 rollups** ÔÇô an important development, as EthereumÔÇÖs on-chain gas fees remain relatively high for retail users. While Ethereum can currently process ~15ÔÇô30 transactions per second, scaling efforts and Layer-2 networks (Arbitrum, Optimism, zkSync, etc.) are gradually increasing throughput and reducing costs. Additionally, EthereumÔÇÖs open development culture has produced token standards to facilitate compliant asset tokens ÔÇô for instance, **ERC-3643**, an Ethereum standard tailored for securities/RWAs that supports regulated transfers and identity checks. Compliance-focused frameworks by companies like **Securitize** build on EthereumÔÇÖs smart contracts to embed KYC/AML rules at the token level (using protocols like ERC-1404/1400 or SecuritizeÔÇÖs DS Protocol). These features allow Ethereum-based RWA tokens to enforce whitelisted investors and geographic restrictions automatically, satisfying regulatory requirements while remaining interoperable with DeFi.
56	
57	### Institutional Adoption on Ethereum
58	
59	Over the past year, Ethereum has seen **accelerating institutional adoption** in tokenization. Beyond BlackRockÔÇÖs fund, other marquee examples include: 
60	
61	- **UBS** ÔÇô In November 2024, UBS Asset Management launched *ÔÇťUBS TokenizeÔÇŁ* and issued **uMINT**, a tokenized money market fund built on Ethereum. This lets clients access a conservative USD cash management fund via Ethereum tokens. UBS developed uMINT as part of SingaporeÔÇÖs **Project Guardian** pilot, working with regulators to ensure compliance. UBS noted growing investor appetite for tokenized assets across asset classes and collaborated with MAS (Monetary Authority of Singapore) to pioneer this regulated offering.
62	
63	- **Franklin Templeton** ÔÇô The $1.4T asset manager expanded its *Franklin OnChain U.S. Government Money Fund* (FOBXX) in 2024 by making its tokenized shares (BENJI tokens) available on multiple networks, including Ethereum and Polygon. By October 2024 the fund reached **$410M** AUM. This SEC-registered fund invests in U.S. Treasuries and allows qualified holders to transfer shares peer-to-peer on-chain. FranklinÔÇÖs initiative ÔÇô begun on Stellar and bridged to EVM chains ÔÇô demonstrates cross-chain flexibility but highlights that **EthereumÔÇÖs ecosystem (e.g. Polygon, Base L2)** is crucial for distribution.
64	
65	- **MakerDAOÔÇÖs RWA Program** ÔÇô MakerDAO (issuer of the DAI stablecoin on Ethereum) has incorporated real-world asset exposure into its reserves at significant scale. By mid-2025, Maker (renamed as *ÔÇťSkyÔÇŁ* in its endgame plan) had deployed **over $3.5B** from its DAI treasury into RWA holdings, including short-term bonds and private credit, in order to back DAI with yield-generating traditional assets. In 2022ÔÇô2023 Maker famously onboarded a **$40M tokenized bond from Soci├ęt├ę G├ęn├ęrale** and invested in pools of loans via partners like **Centrifuge**. This trend continued into 2024, with MakerÔÇÖs on-chain lending arm (Spark) tapping tokenized Treasuries and loans. MakerÔÇÖs RWA strategy shows how **EthereumÔÇÖs DeFi protocols are integrating tokenized assets**: regulated trustees hold real assets off-chain while ERC-20 tokens representing those assets are used as collateral on-chain. This blend of DeFi and TradFi on Ethereum is unlocking new sources of liquidity and yield.
66	
67	- **Other Institutional Highlights** ÔÇô *BlackRockÔÇÖs* BUIDL fund on Ethereum (noted above) crossed **$1 billion in assets within months**. *JPMorgan* executed a pilot in late 2022 for tokenized yen and Singapore dollar deposits on a modified Ethereum Layer-2 (Project Guardian Phase I), and in 2024 it continued exploring on-chain bonds and collateral. *Fidelity, WisdomTree, and VanEck* have each launched tokenized funds or ETFs on Ethereum or Ethereum-based networks ÔÇô e.g. WisdomTreeÔÇÖs **tokenized gold (WTGOLD)** on Ethereum and VanEckÔÇÖs **tokenized Treasury fund (VBILL)**. Even traditional exchanges began experiments: **SwitzerlandÔÇÖs SIX** and GermanyÔÇÖs **Deutsche B├Ârse** have utilized Ethereum or Ethereum-based permissioned chains for bond issuance and trading pilots. These examples underscore that Ethereum is often the **default choice for institutions** due to its robust infrastructure and acceptance. Importantly, a broad range of asset types are now on Ethereum: from **tokenized private credit** (the largest segment of RWAs, ~$14B by mid-2025) to **commodity tokens** like gold (e.g. PaxosÔÇÖ PAXG, market cap $500M+) and even **tokenized equities** (e.g. **SecuritizeÔÇÖs** tokens for private equity funds by KKR and Hamilton Lane issued on Ethereum in 2023). 
68	
69	EthereumÔÇÖs credibility has even led to new initiatives to maintain its lead. In January 2025, a consortium led by former Wall Street veterans launched **ÔÇťEtherealize,ÔÇŁ** an outreach initiative to position Ethereum as the ÔÇť**digital oil**ÔÇŁ powering the tokenized financial system. This represents a strategic push to coordinate EthereumÔÇÖs traditionally diffuse community towards meeting institutional needs. While EthereumÔÇÖs decentralized governance can make rapid business development challenging, its vast community of developers and **largest DeFi liquidity pools** give it an entrenched advantage. At ~**76,000 RWA holders** (mid-2025), Ethereum also has the widest distribution of tokenized asset investors ÔÇô ranging from large institutions to crypto-savvy retail users around the world.
70	
71	### Retail Adoption and Use Cases on Ethereum
72	
73	From the retail investor perspective, Ethereum offers access to many tokenized assets, though often with some barriers (like KYC or high gas fees). Notably, **fractional real estate** has been a pioneering retail use case on Ethereum. **RealT**, a platform launched in 2019, uses Ethereum to sell tokenized equity in rental properties (primarily in the U.S.), enabling investors globally to buy fractions of houses for as little as ~$50. As of 2024, RealT had tokenized **200+ properties** on Ethereum, with token holders earning proportional rental income. This democratizes real estate investing ÔÇô a user can own 0.1% of a house and trade that ownership on secondary markets. RealTÔÇÖs tokens adhere to U.S. securities exemptions (Reg D/Reg S), meaning investors undergo KYC and non-US retail can participate, showing how EthereumÔÇÖs public network can handle compliance for retail-facing RWAs. 
74	
75	Another retail-friendly RWA on Ethereum is **Pax Gold (PAXG)**. Each PAXG token represents one troy ounce of physical gold stored in London vaults, and holders can redeem tokens for real gold bars. Launched in 2019, PAXG grew to be the largest tokenized commodity with **over $500M market cap in 2024**, held by a mix of retail users and institutions as a convenient way to get gold exposure on-chain. Its success highlighted retail appetite for asset-backed tokens that are easier to transfer than the underlying asset (in this case, gold bullion). Other examples of **commodity tokenization** on Ethereum include DigixÔÇÖs gold token and tokenized **diamonds** (e.g. Diamond StandardÔÇÖs tokens).
76	
77	Despite these opportunities, **EthereumÔÇÖs high transaction fees have been a pain point for retail**. During busy periods in 2024, a single ERC-20 token transfer could cost a few dollars in gas ÔÇô a significant cost if one is investing only $100. This has driven some retail-focused RWA projects to seek scaling solutions. RealT, for instance, migrated a lot of activity to an Ethereum sidechain (xDai/Gnosis Chain) to facilitate cheaper transactions for its users while still using EthereumÔÇÖs security model for finality. In the broader ecosystem, many tokenized assets targeting retail are launching on Ethereum **Layer-2 networks**. For example, in 2023ÔÇô24, multiple startups (like **Backed Finance and Matrixdock**) issued tokenized securities (stocks or bonds) on **Polygon or Arbitrum**, which inherit EthereumÔÇÖs security but offer lower fees for end-users. 
78	
79	Overall, EthereumÔÇÖs retail RWA adoption is growing but tends to involve **niche platforms** and early adopters. The average retail user still faces user-experience challenges (managing wallets, paying gas, passing KYC on multiple portals). However, progress is being made: **account abstraction (ERC-4337)** is enabling more user-friendly ÔÇťsmart wallets,ÔÇŁ and major fintechs like **PayPal** have integrated Ethereum for their own stablecoin (PYUSD) ÔÇô signaling a future where users may interact with tokenized assets on Ethereum via familiar interfaces. As infrastructure improves, EthereumÔÇÖs enormous DeFi ecosystem could become readily accessible to retail participants in RWAs. Already, once a tokenized asset is issued on Ethereum, **DeFi protocols can integrate it** ÔÇô for example, RWA tokens (with proper permissions) can be used in lending platforms or AMMs, providing liquidity and yield opportunities that benefit retail investors. This composability is a unique strength of Ethereum: even regulated assets can potentially plug into on-chain money markets (under whitelisted conditions) to amplify their utility. 
80	
81	In summary, **EthereumÔÇÖs fundamental investment case is bolstered by its role as the primary platform for tokenizing real-world assets**. High-profile institutional adoption reinforces EthereumÔÇÖs position at the center of on-chain finance, which in turn drives demand for Ether (ETH) for transaction fees and staking. At the same time, EthereumÔÇÖs community continues to address its challenges (scalability, cost) through technical upgrades and Layer-2 expansion. If even a single-digit percentage of the $400T traditional asset market eventually moves on-chain, Ethereum stands to capture a substantial portion given its head start ÔÇô a prospect that underpins a bullish long-term outlook (absent any explicit price predictions). However, Ethereum is no longer the only game in town for RWAs, as weÔÇÖll see with SolanaÔÇÖs rise.
82	
83	## Solana: The High-Performance Challenger for Tokenization
84	
85	Solana has reemerged as a serious challenger in the tokenization arena, particularly in 2024 and 2025. After navigating a difficult 2022 (which saw network outages and the FTX collapse impacting ecosystem sentiment), Solana refocused on its core strengths: **high throughput, low latency, and ultra-low fees**. The networkÔÇÖs capability to process **1,000+ transactions per second in real conditions** with **400 millisecond block times** and gas fees around **$0.0001ÔÇô$0.01** is a key differentiator. These features make Solana naturally suited for tokenized asset use cases that involve **high-volume trading or real-time settlement**, such as **foreign exchange, money markets, or retail equity trading**. By contrast, executing such high-frequency transactions on Ethereum would be prohibitively expensive and slow for most users. SolanaÔÇÖs technical architecture (a single global state without sharding) ensures that all assets and programs reside on one shard, simplifying composability and **avoiding the fragmentation** that can occur in multi-chain or sharded environments. This ÔÇťsingle source of truthÔÇŁ design is attractive for financial institutions who require consistent, fast execution and prefer not to deal with complex cross-chain bridges for liquidity. 
86	
87	### Technology and Network Upgrades
88	
89	Several **technological developments** have strengthened SolanaÔÇÖs appeal for tokenization. First, the network dramatically improved its stability in 2023ÔÇô2024. Software upgrades (like QUIC-based networking and localized fee markets) and better node management reduced the outage incidents that plagued Solana in early 2022. By mid-2024, Solana demonstrated much **improved reliability** ÔÇô a necessary condition to win back institutional trust. Furthermore, **Jump CryptoÔÇÖs ÔÇťFire DancerÔÇŁ** project is set to deliver a second independent Solana validator client (expected in 2024ÔÇô2025), which will not only bolster decentralization but also significantly boost performance (preliminary tests demonstrated up to 600k TPS in a lab setting). Having multiple node implementations reduces the risk of single points of failure in SolanaÔÇÖs software, addressing prior concerns about network halts.
90	
91	Crucially for RWAs, SolanaÔÇÖs developers have introduced **built-in compliance and custody features** at the protocol level. The **Solana Program Library (SPL) token standard** now supports *ÔÇťtoken extensionsÔÇŁ* that can enforce **transfer restrictions, freezes, and role-based controls** on assets. For example, an issuer can mint an SPL token with an authority that can **freeze or claw back tokens**, enabling compliance with court orders or regulatory requirements. Solana also supports *ÔÇťconfidential tokensÔÇŁ* where transaction amounts can be hidden (using encryption via ElGamal), potentially useful for privacy in financial trades. Additionally, SolanaÔÇÖs single-shard execution allows smart contracts to easily check identities or whitelists on-chain before allowing a token transfer ÔÇô thus automating **ÔÇťprogrammable complianceÔÇŁ** for securities. As a result, Solana can offer institutions a robust **RWA protocol** for secure and compliant tokenization, where asset legitimacy and investor qualifications are verified within the system. This contrasts with EthereumÔÇÖs approach where such compliance is usually handled by external smart contract logic or off-chain services. SolanaÔÇÖs more **vertically integrated approach** (layer-1 support for permissioned environments and identity) has started to resonate with enterprises looking for turnkey solutions.
92	
93	To cater to enterprise users, the Solana ecosystem has also embraced **ÔÇťpermissioned instancesÔÇŁ** of its technology. For instance, projects like **Sphere** and others (with Solana Foundation support) are exploring **Solana Enterprise (SPE)** chains ÔÇô essentially private or permissioned Solana networks that can connect to the public Solana for liquidity. In 2025, Solana announced a partnership with **R3 Corda**, a leading enterprise DLT platform used by banks. This integration aims to let institutions use CordaÔÇÖs private ledgers for internal processes, while **tapping SolanaÔÇÖs public network for broader liquidity or settlement**. ItÔÇÖs a hybrid model offering ÔÇťthe best of both worldsÔÇŁ ÔÇô banks keep sensitive workflows in a permissioned environment but settle or list assets on SolanaÔÇÖs open market for wider access. Such innovations signal to institutional clients that Solana is willing to meet them halfway on regulatory and operational needs.
94	
95	### Institutional Adoption on Solana
96	
97	In 2024ÔÇô2025, Solana saw **significant institutional inroads**, reflected in rapid growth of tokenized assets on the network. While starting from a smaller base, the **tokenized RWA market on Solana expanded ~218% in 2025 alone**. By mid-2025 SolanaÔÇÖs on-chain RWA value was **$351M**, making it the 4th-largest public chain for RWAs (behind Ethereum and one or two others like zkSync and Aptos). Several high-profile initiatives contributed to this momentum:
98	
99	- **Ondo FinanceÔÇÖs Tokenized Treasuries:** Perhaps the biggest driver has been **Ondo Finance**, which deployed its tokenized U.S. Treasury offerings on Solana. Ondo issues tokens like **USDY (Ondo USD Yield)** and **OUSG (Ondo Short-Term T-Bill Fund)**, which represent shares in portfolios of Treasury bills yielding around 4ÔÇô5%. According to RWA data, **OndoÔÇÖs products account for $248M** of SolanaÔÇÖs RWA market by mid-2025. These tokens are offered to qualified investors (primarily outside the U.S.) and provide an on-chain instrument akin to a money market fund. SolanaÔÇÖs low fees and fast finality make it feasible to trade or arbitrage these ÔÇťon-chain TreasuriesÔÇŁ frequently ÔÇô an attractive feature for institutional traders and liquidity providers. The presence of such large, low-risk assets also bootstrapped SolanaÔÇÖs RWA TVL, as investors seeking stable yield moved capital onto the network.
100	
101	- **Apollo Global ManagementÔÇÖs Credit Fund (ACRED):** A milestone for Solana was **Apollo Global ManagementÔÇÖs first on-chain fund offering**, known as **ACRED** (a tokenized feeder into ApolloÔÇÖs $1.3B diversified private credit fund). Apollo, a $500+ billion asset manager, partnered with digital securities platform **Securitize** to issue ACRED as a security token. Notably, the **ACRED tokens were made available on multiple blockchains including Solana**. By mid-2025, ApolloÔÇÖs ACRED had a presence on SolanaÔÇÖs network, signifying **unprecedented institutional capital coming onto Solana**. The Solana implementation (often labeled sACRED when wrapped for DeFi) enabled ApolloÔÇÖs fund tokens to be used within SolanaÔÇÖs DeFi ecosystem under whitelisted conditions. For example, Solana-based protocols like **Drift (a derivatives DEX) and Kamino (an automated yield optimizer)** started integrating sACRED tokens, demonstrating that institutional assets could interact with DeFi on Solana. The **Apollo case is pivotal** ÔÇô it showed that a TradFi giant is comfortable leveraging Solana for distribution and even for on-chain leverage strategies (Gauntlet Network built a leveraged yield strategy around ApolloÔÇÖs token on Solana). This validation has attracted attention from other asset managers.
102	
103	- **Partnerships & Infrastructure:** SolanaÔÇÖs ecosystem took proactive steps to **court institutional players**. The Solana Foundation hosted an **Accelerate conference in New York (late 2024)** specifically to showcase institutional-grade RWA use cases, signaling a coordinated strategy. Custody providers like **Anchorage Digital (a federally chartered crypto bank)** integrated Solana, offering custody and settlement services for Solana RWAs ÔÇô a critical requirement for institutional adoption. On the banking side, **J.P. MorganÔÇÖs Onyx** and other fintech platforms have explored Solana for potential projects (Onyx integrated a prototype collateral blockchain with multiple networks, though not confirmed on Solana yet). Another example is **Superstate** ÔÇô a startup led by DeFi veteran Haseeb Qureshi ÔÇô which chose Solana for its flagship platform ÔÇťOpening Bell,ÔÇŁ aiming to tokenize traditional assets like **public equities and investment funds**. In mid-2025, Superstate partnered with a **Nasdaq-listed company (Upexi, Inc.)** to pilot the **tokenization of common stock shares on Solana**. Upexi plans to list a class of its SEC-registered shares on Solana via SuperstateÔÇÖs regulated infrastructure, making them tradeable as on-chain equity tokens. This initiative, which would be one of the first of its kind, is possible in part because Solana can handle the anticipated trading volume of equities and offers near-instant settlement, aligning with equity market needs. Upexi even disclosed that it had accumulated **735,000 SOL tokens (~$100M)** in its corporate treasury, reflecting confidence in SolanaÔÇÖs future.
104	
105	- **Other Notable Projects:** Additional Solana RWA projects include **Maple Finance**, an institutional lending platform originally on Ethereum that expanded onto Solana. Maple relaunched on Solana in 2023ÔÇô24 to facilitate **tokenized credit pools (corporate loans) funded by accredited lenders**, taking advantage of SolanaÔÇÖs efficiency for higher-frequency interest payments and updates. ThereÔÇÖs also **Homebase (real estate)** which we cover under retail, and burgeoning initiatives in areas like **trade finance** or **invoice factoring** using SolanaÔÇÖs tech (e.g., **Credix** ÔÇô which operates on Solana to fund Latin American loans ÔÇô though its tokens often bridge to Ethereum, it demonstrates SolanaÔÇÖs use in credit markets). 
106	
107	Overall, SolanaÔÇÖs strategy seems to be working: by mid-2025, it hosts **16 distinct RWA products** (vs. 300+ on Ethereum) but those include some of the largest on-chain funds by AUM. SolanaÔÇÖs share of the RWA pie (3ÔÇô4%) is still small, but itÔÇÖs growing faster in percentage terms than EthereumÔÇÖs. The **fundamental investment case for Solana** is strengthened by these developments ÔÇô they indicate rising network utilization by credible players. If Solana becomes the go-to chain for high-speed financial instruments (like money markets and stock trading), demand for SOL (for transaction fees and staking collateral) could increase correspondingly, assuming the network continues to prove its robustness.
108	
109	### Retail Adoption and Use Cases on Solana
110	
111	SolanaÔÇÖs low costs and user-friendly design have enabled some compelling **retail-focused tokenization use cases**, often targeting scenarios where Ethereum was less practical. A prime example is **Homebase**, a startup that has been **tokenizing single-family rental homes in the U.S.** on Solana. In March 2023, Homebase sold tokenized ownership in a **$235,000 house in Texas to 38 investors** via Solana, raising ~$246k (with a small surplus for maintenance) in just two weeks. Investors received NFTs representing shares in an LLC that owns the property, entitling them to rental income and a cut of any future sale. Notably, **about 78% of HomebaseÔÇÖs investors were non-institutional (retail)**, with a minimum investment of only $500. This demonstrates real uptake by everyday people, many of whom were completely new to crypto ÔÇô Homebase reported that 15 of the 38 buyers for the first property **created their first-ever crypto wallet (on Solana) just for this purchase**. By mid-2023, Homebase completed a second similar home tokenization, confirming that the model was replicable.
112	
113	Why did Homebase choose Solana? According to the founders, they sought a **seamless user experience** for non-crypto natives. Solana offered **fast, feeless transactions (in practice) and smooth USDC stablecoin integration**, which was crucial for giving investors confidence. Homebase built a compliance layer using SolanaÔÇÖs **Metaplex NFT protocol** to wrap the tokens with legal controls: they implemented smart contract features to **enforce a one-year lockup (for Reg D rules), KYC gating, and allow a regulated entity to freeze or reissue tokens if needed**. This level of compliance tooling was possible on Solana without sacrificing ease-of-use. The team also cited EthereumÔÇÖs downsides for their use case ÔÇô volatile gas fees and complex L2 bridging were non-starters for onboarding first-time users. In their words, *ÔÇťbridging from a Layer 2 blockchain is quite complexÔÇŽthereÔÇÖs a period where your money just disappears [in transit], not great for investor confidenceÔÇŁ*. SolanaÔÇÖs direct, fast on-chain settlement in USDC overcame these hurdles. As a result, Homebase could offer a web app where users sign up, pass KYC, deposit dollars (converted to USDC), and purchase real estate tokens, all in a few clicks ÔÇô with the crypto underpinnings invisible in the background. This kind of **integrated Web2.5 e

37	## 2) Differentiation vs competitors (what makes Edelweiss ÔÇťdifferentÔÇŁ)

39	### A) The core differentiator: a ÔÇťplatform + monetizationÔÇŁ model (vs pure-play lenders/brokers)

45	Edelweiss is unusual in that it has repeatedly **incubated businesses, scaled them, and then monetized stakes** to generate liquidity and manage leverage. ICRA highlights the **sale of controlling stake in wealth management (later Nuvama) in FY2021**, **sale of insurance broking in FY2022**, and **monetization of residual stake in Nuvama in FY2025** to generate **>Ôé╣3,200 crore liquidity**, demonstrating willingness to monetize to reduce debt/strengthen balance sheet. ţłÇciteţłéturn13view0ţłü

47	This ÔÇťincubate Ôćĺ scale Ôćĺ monetize/partially listÔÇŁ approach is a key differentiation, but it also creates a **holding-company complexity discount** (more on that in the stock section).

62	## 3) Lending and investing practices (what the sources imply)

66	- The Group has **discontinued wholesale lending from NBFCs** and is **running down the wholesale book**. ţłÇciteţłéturn13view0ţłü

68	- The remaining ÔÇťvulnerable portfolioÔÇŁ (including stressed exposures) was still meaningful: **Ôé╣6,174 crore as on Sep 30, 2025**. ţłÇciteţłéturn13view0ţłü

70	**Interpretation for stockholders:** this is a de-risking story *in progress*. The market will usually reward ÔÇťdeclining tail riskÔÇŁ only if recoveries and leverage reduction are sustained and transparent.

72	### B) Investing practices: two very different risk engines

93	## 4) Employee culture (what can be inferred, and what cannot)

109	
110	### A) Deleveraging is a stated priority
111	Company materials show a plan to reduce **corporate net debt** and target near-zero over ~3 years:

124	- **WestBridge** acquiring **15%** of the mutual fund business for **Ôé╣450 crore** (approvals/process described by the company). ţłÇciteţłéturn7view0ţłéturn13view0ţłü  
125	
126	### C) Insurance: aiming for breakeven, but still a drag today
127	The investor presentation indicates insurance losses declining and states insurance businesses are on track to breakeven by FY27. ţłÇciteţłéturn7view0ţłü  
128	
129	---
130	
131	## 6) Stock movement analysis (Ôëł18 years of public trading history)
132	
133	### Important limitation on ÔÇť20 yearsÔÇŁ
134	Edelweiss was listed in **December 2007**, so you can analyze roughly **Dec 2007 Ôćĺ Dec 2025** as public-market history. ţłÇciteţłéturn4search21ţłéturn16view0ţłü  
135	
136	### A) Where the stock is now (latest in provided sources)
137	StockAnalysis shows **Ôé╣108.17** at close on **Dec 26, 2025**. ţłÇciteţłéturn19view0ţłü  
138	
139	### B) Annual price performance since listing (2007ÔÇô2025)
140	(From CompaniesMarketCapÔÇÖs annual performance series.) ţłÇciteţłéturn16view0ţłü  
141	
142	| Year | % |
143	|---:|---:|
144	| 2007 | 7.32% |
145	| 2008 | -86.17% |
146	| 2009 | 73.35% |
147	| 2010 | -1.12% |
148	| 2011 | -53.00% |
149	| 2012 | 32.66% |
150	| 2013 | -26.09% |
151	| 2014 | 86.32% |
152	| 2015 | 6.49% |
153	| 2016 | 71.20% |
154	| 2017 | 225.18% |
155	| 2018 | -42.70% |
156	| 2019 | -40.71% |
157	| 2020 | -39.43% |
158	| 2021 | 5.98% |
159	| 2022 | -20.61% |
160	| 2023 | 133.35% |
161	| 2024 | 54.91% |
162	| 2025 | -15.86% |
163	
164	### C) What this implies (cycle interpretation)
165	Using the annual series above (price returns only):
166	
167	- **High volatility / deep drawdowns** are a defining feature:
168	  - Worst year: **2008 (-86%)** ţłÇciteţłéturn16view0ţłü  
169	  - Another major stress: **2018ÔÇô2020 cumulative Ôëł -79%** (three consecutive ~-40% years). ţłÇciteţłéturn16view0ţłü  
170	
171	- **Big rerating phases exist**, usually when (a) balance sheet risk looks contained and/or (b) franchise value becomes visible:
172	  - Best year: **2017 (+225%)** ţłÇciteţłéturn16view0ţłü  
173	  - Strong rebound: **2023ÔÇô2024 cumulative Ôëł +261%** ţłÇciteţłéturn16view0ţłü  
174	
175	- **Long-run price return since 2007 in this series is not compounding smoothly**: compounding 2007ÔÇô2025 annual returns yields an overall factor of about **0.68x** (roughly **-32%** cumulative price change; dividends not included). ţłÇciteţłéturn16view0ţłü  
176	  This is consistent with a company that went through a major **leverage/stress cycle** and later transitioned strategy (deleveraging + value unlocking), as reflected in ICRAÔÇÖs emphasis on monetization and portfolio vulnerability reduction. ţłÇciteţłéturn13view0ţłü  
177	
178	**Key linkage to fundamentals:**  
179	- The 2018ÔÇô2020 collapse aligns directionally with the period where the Indian NBFC space faced severe liquidity/credit concerns and where Edelweiss had meaningful wholesale/stressed exposure that later needed run-down/markdowns (ICRAÔÇÖs discussion of stressed wholesale vulnerability and the ARC book is consistent with why the equity would be volatile). ţłÇciteţłéturn13view0ţłü  
180	- The 2023ÔÇô2024 rebound is consistent with the market rewarding **deleveraging actions and clarity on value-unlocking** (e.g., monetization of Nuvama stake; proposed stake sale in AMC; pathway to EAAA IPO). ţłÇciteţłéturn13view0ţłéturn7view0ţłü  
181	
182	---
183	
184	## 7) Investor ownership and Mohnish PabraiÔÇÖs influence
185	
186	### A) Current ownership structure (as of Sep 30, 2025)
187	Company presentation shows:
188	- **Promoter group:** **32.7%**  
189	- **Management:** **0.6%**  
190	- **Foreign institutions & companies:** **19.2%**  
191	- **DIIs / non-institutions / others:** **47.5%** ţłÇciteţłéturn11view0ţłü  
192	
193	A third-party compilation (Groww) reports a similar breakdown for the same period range. ţłÇciteţłéturn4search30ţłü  
194	
195	### B) Key shareholders list includes Pabrai Investment Funds
196	In the ÔÇťkey shareholdersÔÇŁ list (Sep 30, 2025), **Pabrai Investment Funds** is shown at **~1.5%** holding, alongside institutions like TIAA CREF (4.0%), LIC (2.6%), Vanguard (2.4%), etc. ţłÇciteţłéturn11view0ţłü  
197	
198	### C) PabraiÔÇÖs entry/visibility
199	Livemint reported in **December 2020** that **Mohnish PabraiÔÇÖs fund bought ~2% stake** in Edelweiss Financial Services through the open market. ţłÇciteţłéturn4search1ţłü  
200	
201	### D) ÔÇťInfluence on the stockÔÇŁ ÔÇö what is realistic
202	With ~**1.5%** reported in Sep 2025, Pabrai is a **meaningful minority investor**, but:
203	- He does **not** control governance (promoters ~32.7% plus broad institutional/free float). ţłÇciteţłéturn11view0ţłü  
204	- His main influence tends to be **second-order**:
205	  - **Signaling / attention:** A well-known value investor taking a stake can attract incremental investor attention and sometimes improve liquidity/near-term sentiment.
206	  - **No direct operational control:** The stockÔÇÖs longer-run path will still be driven by execution on corporate debt reduction, recoveries on stressed exposures, and scaling fee businesses (AMC/EAAA), all of which are explicitly highlighted as core drivers in company disclosures and ICRA commentary. ţłÇciteţłéturn13view0ţłéturn7view0ţłü  
207	
208	---
209	
210	## 8) What to watch going forward (stock drivers vs risks)
211	
212	### Potential positive drivers
213	- **Execution of EAAA IPO** (timeline signaled around Apr 2026) and whether it achieves ÔÇťplatform multipleÔÇŁ valuation. ţłÇciteţłéturn7view0ţłü  
214	- **Completion of AMC stake sale to WestBridge** and subsequent governance/flow momentum in mutual funds. ţłÇciteţłéturn7view0ţłéturn13view0ţłü  
215	- **Sustained reduction in corporate net debt** (management plan described) and lower financing drag. ţłÇciteţłéturn7view0ţłéturn13view0ţłü  
216	- **Recoveries from stressed exposures / ARC outcomes** becoming less uncertain and less dominant in headline profitability. ţłÇciteţłéturn13view0ţłü  
217	
218	### Core risks (structural)
219	- **Residual portfolio vulnerability** and uncertainty of recovery timing/amounts. ţłÇciteţłéturn13view0ţłü  
220	- **Holding-company complexity/discount** if listings/stake sales do not translate into visible per-share value and lower leverage. ţłÇciteţłéturn13view0ţłéturn7view0ţłü  
221	- **Regulatory shifts** impacting the alternatives ecosystem and bank/NBFC participation in AIFs. ţłÇciteţłéturn6news57ţłü  
222

1	# HBM4 breakthroughs and yields
2	
3	Source: ChatGPT | Date: 2025-11-27
4	
5	HBM4 is only just moving from samples into early production, so public dataÔÇöespecially on yieldsÔÇöis sparse and often indirect. Below is a synthesis of what is reasonably grounded in public sources plus an explicit, modelled revenue / share scenario.
6	
7	---
8	
9	## 1. HBM4 status and yields by vendor (late 2025)
10	
11	### Technology baseline
12	
13	- JEDECÔÇÖs HBM4 standard (JESD238) was finalized in April 2025. It targets up to ~2ÔÇ»TB/s per stack, higher capacities (up to 64ÔÇ»GB/stack) and major powerÔÇĹefficiency gains vs HBM3E.ţłÇciteţłéturn0search9ţłéturn0search13ţłü  
14	- All three major suppliers (SK hynix, Samsung, Micron) are converging on 24ÔÇ»Gb DRAM dies, 12ÔÇĹHi and 16ÔÇĹHi stacks, and custom logic/base dies on advanced foundry nodes (TSMC 12FFC+/N5, Samsung 4ÔÇ»nm).ţłÇciteţłéturn8search2ţłéturn1search1ţłü  
15	- Industry roadmaps place HBM4 mass production in 2026, with HBM4EÔÇĹclass parts becoming the dominant HBM generation around 2027ÔÇô2028.ţłÇciteţłéturn5view0ţłéturn3news42ţłü  
16	
17	#### A note on ÔÇťyieldsÔÇŁ
18	
19	Reported ÔÇťyieldsÔÇŁ can mean:
20	
21	- FrontÔÇĹend DRAM die yield (per wafer)  
22	- Logic/baseÔÇĹdie yield  
23	- Final stacked HBM yield (all dies + TSV + packaging)
24	
25	Stack yields are much lower than individual die yields; for example, one analysis shows that 95ÔÇ»% perÔÇĹdie yield can still drop to ~44ÔÇ»% final yield on 16ÔÇĹHi stacks.ţłÇciteţłéturn6search6ţłü
26	
27	---
28	
29	### SK hynix
30	
31	Position: Clear HBM leader, already into HBM4 production ramp.
32	
33	Key HBM4 breakthroughs
34	
35	- Announced completion of HBM4 development in 2025; specs include up to 10ÔÇ»Gb/s per pin and Ôëł40ÔÇ»% better power efficiency than HBM3E.ţłÇciteţłéturn3search10ţłéturn1search3ţłü  
36	- Shipped HBM4 samples from March 2025, completed internal certification, and is preparing full mass production; major supplier to NVIDIA.ţłÇciteţłéturn3news42ţłéturn1news41ţłü  
37	- Roadmap (2026ÔÇĹ2028) adds 16ÔÇĹHi HBM4 and multiple HBM4E variants (8/12/16ÔÇĹHi), including custom baseÔÇĹdie designs that integrate the memory controller to free GPU area and cut interface power.ţłÇciteţłéturn8search14ţłü  
38	
39	Yields (publicly visible signals)
40	
41	- For HBM3E, SK hynix has reported production yields around 80ÔÇ»%, enabled by its MRÔÇĹMUF (Mass Reflow Molded Underfill) stacking process, explicitly framed as paving the way for HBM4 scalability.ţłÇciteţłéturn2search14ţłü  
42	- SK hynixÔÇÖs 1cÔÇĹnode DRAM (for future HBM4E) has been reported with test yields averaging >80ÔÇ»% and up to 90ÔÇ»% on some lots; HBM4 itself initially uses the more mature 1b node.ţłÇciteţłéturn2search3ţłéturn2search8ţłü  
43	- Analysts and press commentary consistently state that SK hynix enjoys the best effective HBM yields among the big three (HBM is ÔÇťmarginÔÇĹaccretiveÔÇŁ for SK hynix), while SamsungÔÇÖs are notably worse.ţłÇciteţłéturn6search14ţłéturn2search8ţłü  
44	
45	TakeÔÇĹaway: SK hynix enters HBM4 with mature packaging, strong HBM3E yields, and firstÔÇĹtoÔÇĹmarket status. It is widely expected to retain the largest HBM4 share through at least 2027ÔÇô2028.ţłÇciteţłéturn3news42ţłéturn2news41ţłü  
46	
47	---
48	
49	### Samsung
50	
51	Position: Very large DRAM maker, but playing catchÔÇĹup in HBM yields and NVIDIA qualification.
52	
53	Key HBM4 breakthroughs
54	
55	- Samsung is delivering HBM4 samples to NVIDIA (Nov 2025) and aims for earlyÔÇĹ2026 validation; volume production is targeted to align with nextÔÇĹgen GPUs.ţłÇciteţłéturn1search4ţłéturn2search4ţłéturn1search0ţłü  
56	- Uses 1cÔÇĹnode DRAM and a 4ÔÇ»nm logic/base die for HBM4, enabling tight integration and leveraging its foundry for custom logic dies.ţłÇciteţłéturn2search13ţłéturn2search5ţłéturn8search2ţłü  
57	- Reported to have significantly improved its AIÔÇĹmemory competitiveness in 2025, with aggressive expansion plans for HBM3E and HBM4 capacity as AI demand continues to exceed supply.ţłÇciteţłéturn6news41ţłéturn1search2ţłü  
58	
59	Yields
60	
61	- 1c DRAM used for HBM4 reportedly has sample yields around 50ÔÇ»% for HBM4 dies, with DDR5 yields above 70ÔÇ»%. Industry sources say Samsung wants Ôëł70ÔÇ»% HBM4 wafer yield before full mass production.ţłÇciteţłéturn2search5ţłéturn2search9ţłéturn2search3ţłü  
62	- SamsungÔÇÖs 4ÔÇ»nm HBM4 logic die reportedly exceeds 90ÔÇ»% yield, indicating the frontÔÇĹend logic is not the bottleneck.ţłÇciteţłéturn2search13ţłü  
63	- Earlier, SamsungÔÇÖs HBM3/HBM3E struggled to pass NVIDIAÔÇÖs tests due to heat and power issues, highlighting that its effective stack yields and systemÔÇĹlevel performance lagged SK hynix through 2024; that gap appears to be narrowing in late 2025.ţłÇciteţłéturn2search0ţłéturn2search4ţłéturn1search9ţłü  
64	
65	TakeÔÇĹaway: SamsungÔÇÖs HBM4 technology is competitive on paper, but effective yields and customer qualifications have trailed SK hynix. The trajectory in 2025 suggests improving yields, with room for share gains once HBM4 is in full volume.
66	
67	---
68	
69	### Micron
70	
71	Position: Late to HBM3, now very aggressive on HBM3E and HBM4; strong with NVIDIA on HBM3E and positioning HBM4E for 2027.
72	
73	Key HBM4 breakthroughs
74	
75	- Micron announced shipment of HBM4 samples in midÔÇĹ2025: 2048ÔÇĹbit interface, >2.0ÔÇ»TB/s bandwidth per stack, >60ÔÇ»% better performance vs its prior HBM, targeting nextÔÇĹgen AI platforms.ţłÇciteţłéturn1search5ţłéturn6news40ţłü  
76	- FollowÔÇĹup disclosures and roadmaps show pin speeds above 11ÔÇ»Gb/s and bandwidth up to Ôëł2.8ÔÇ»TB/s per stack, exceeding the JEDEC baseline.ţłÇciteţłéturn1search1ţłéturn6search1ţłéturn6search5ţłü  
77	- MicronÔÇÖs HBM4E (2027) will use custom baseÔÇĹlogic dies fabricated by TSMC, enabling customerÔÇĹspecific variants with higher gross margins.ţłÇciteţłéturn5view0ţłéturn6search5ţłéturn6search8ţłü  
78	
79	Yields
80	
81	- Multiple analyses (SemiAnalysis, investor notes) characterize MicronÔÇÖs HBM3E yields as competitive with or better than SK hynix and materially ahead of Samsung, enough that HBM is ÔÇťmarginÔÇĹaccretiveÔÇŁ despite lower absolute yields than commodity DRAM.ţłÇciteţłéturn6search14ţłéturn6search3ţłü  
82	- There are reports that Micron has had to redesign parts of its HBM4 due to yield/performance issues, potentially pushing some volume out toward 2027; this is framed as a temporary setback rather than a structural disadvantage.ţłÇciteţłéturn6search0ţłéturn6search13ţłü  
83	- No hard HBM4 yield numbers have been disclosed; based on HBM3E experience, it is reasonable to assume Micron aims for effective stack yields in the 50ÔÇô70ÔÇ»% range once HBM4 is fully ramped.
84	
85	TakeÔÇĹaway: Micron appears technologically competitive (fastest advertised HBM4 bandwidth today), but its HBM4 ramp is likely a bit later than SK hynix and Samsung. Its share gains are more visible in HBM3E, with HBM4/4E becoming a larger revenue driver from 2027 onward.
86	
87	---
88	
89	### Chinese vendors (CXMT, YMTC, Huawei ecosystem)
90	
91	There are no public indications that any Chinese vendor will massÔÇĹproduce JEDECÔÇĹcompliant HBM4 in the 2026ÔÇô2027 timeframe. Current efforts are focused on HBM3/HBM3EÔÇĹequivalent tech and inÔÇĹhouse/custom HBM for domestic AI accelerators.
92	
93	Key points
94	
95	- CXMT plans mass production of HBM3 around 2026 using MRÔÇĹMUF packaging, explicitly following SK hynixÔÇÖs process, and is targeting HBM3E around 2027; it remains ~3ÔÇô4 years behind the leaders.ţłÇciteţłéturn3search0ţłéturn3search6ţłéturn3search7ţłü  
96	- YMTC (primarily a NAND maker) is entering DRAM and HBM, developing TSVÔÇĹbased stacking and exploring partnership with CXMT for HBM.ţłÇciteţłéturn3search3ţłéturn3search4ţłéturn0search15ţłü  
97	- Huawei has announced its own HiBL 1.0 HBM for the AscendÔÇ»950PR (2026), claiming 128ÔÇ»GB and up to 1.6ÔÇ»TB/s bandwidth and saying it is more costÔÇĹeffective than industry HBM3E/HBM4E; a secondÔÇĹgen ÔÇťHiZQÔÇ»2.0ÔÇŁ is planned with 144ÔÇ»GB and up to 4ÔÇ»TB/s.ţłÇciteţłéturn3search2ţłéturn3search5ţłéturn3search8ţłéturn8search3ţłéturn8search15ţłü  
98	- Export controls on advanced tools and packaging are visibly constraining YMTC and other Chinese memory vendors; yields on leadingÔÇĹedge products made with domestic tools are widely reported to be lower than international peers.ţłÇciteţłéturn8news40ţłéturn3search4ţłü  
99	
100	TakeÔÇĹaway: Chinese players are unlikely to have material global HBM4 share before ~2028+, though they may play a growing role in the domestic China AI stack (Huawei Ascend, etc.). Their first volume products are more realistically HBM3/HBM3EÔÇĹclass.
101	
102	---
103	
104	## 2. Expected revenue contribution and market share for HBM4
105	
106	Because HBM4 is just ramping, any forward view has to be scenarioÔÇĹbased. The charts I generated above use a scenario primarily consistent with Yole GroupÔÇÖs aggressive HBM forecasts and TrendForceÔÇÖs HBM4E adoption timeline.
107	
108	### 2.1 Global HBM vs HBM4 revenue ÔÇô scenario
109	
110	Several recent forecasts:
111	
112	- Some market studies put HBM revenue at only ~US$2.5ÔÇô3ÔÇ»B in 2024, reaching ~US$5ÔÇô6ÔÇ»B by 2030 (very conservative).ţłÇciteţłéturn0search2ţłéturn4search11ţłéturn0search14ţłü  
113	- YoleÔÇĹbased work cited by multiple outlets instead projects HBM revenue growing from about US$17ÔÇ»B in 2024 to roughly US$98ÔÇ»B by 2030, a ~33ÔÇ»% CAGR.ţłÇciteţłéturn4search1ţłéturn4search3ţłéturn4search10ţłéturn4search12ţłü  
114	
115	The table and plots here assume the *higher* YoleÔÇĹstyle trajectory:
116	
117	| Year | Total HBM revenue (B$) | HBM4 family share of HBM | HBM4 revenue (B$) |
118	|------|------------------------|--------------------------|-------------------|
119	| 2024 | 17.0 | 0ÔÇ»% | 0.0 |
120	| 2025 | 34.0 | 0ÔÇ»% | 0.0 |
121	| 2026 | 42.0 | 15ÔÇ»% | 6.3 |
122	| 2027 | 51.9 | 60ÔÇ»% | 31.2 |
123	| 2028 | 64.2 | 80ÔÇ»% | 51.3 |
124	| 2029 | 79.3 | 90ÔÇ»% | 71.4 |
125	| 2030 | 98.0 | 95ÔÇ»% | 93.1 |
126	
127	Assumptions:
128	
129	- HBM4 (including HBM4E) begins meaningful volume in 2026, consistent with SK hynix and Samsung roadmaps.ţłÇciteţłéturn3news42ţłéturn1news41ţłéturn5view0ţłü  
130	- TrendForceÔÇÖs estimate that HBM4E alone is ~40ÔÇ»% of HBM demand by 2027 implies that HBM4 + HBM4E together dominate (>60ÔÇ»%) from 2027 onward.ţłÇciteţłéturn5view0ţłü  
131	
132	If instead you adopt the more conservative ÔÇťHBM ~US$6ÔÇ»B in 2030ÔÇŁ forecasts, you can roughly divide all HBM4 revenue numbers above by ~15ÔÇô16; the vendor *shares* below would be similar, but absolute dollars far smaller.
133	
134	---
135	
136	### 2.2 VendorÔÇĹlevel HBM4 revenue and share ÔÇô scenario
137	
138	The two plots I generated show:
139	
140	1. HBM4 revenue by vendor (2026ÔÇô2030)  
141	2. HBM4 market share by vendor (2026ÔÇô2030)
142	
143	The underlying numerical scenario (USD billions):
144	
145	| Year | HBM4 total | SK hynix | Samsung | Micron | ChinaÔÇĹbased vendors |
146	|------|------------|----------|---------|--------|---------------------|
147	| 2026 | 6.3 | 3.18 | 1.56 | 1.50 | 0.06 |
148	| 2027 | 31.2 | 15.27 | 7.63 | 7.63 | 0.62 |
149	| 2028 | 51.3 | 23.41 | 13.45 | 12.95 | 1.54 |
150	| 2029 | 71.4 | 30.15 | 19.18 | 19.18 | 2.85 |
151	| 2030 | 93.1 | 37.15 | 26.53 | 24.76 | 4.66 |
152	
153	Implied HBM4 marketÔÇĹshare paths:
154	
155	- 2026: SK hynix Ôëł50ÔÇ»%, Samsung Ôëł25ÔÇ»%, Micron Ôëł24ÔÇ»%, China Ôëł1ÔÇ»%  
156	- 2027: SK hynix Ôëł49ÔÇ»%, Samsung Ôëł24.5ÔÇ»%, Micron Ôëł24.5ÔÇ»%, China Ôëł2ÔÇ»%  
157	- 2028: SK hynix Ôëł46ÔÇ»%, Samsung Ôëł26ÔÇ»%, Micron Ôëł25ÔÇ»%, China Ôëł3ÔÇ»%  
158	- 2029: SK hynix Ôëł42ÔÇ»%, Samsung Ôëł27ÔÇ»%, Micron Ôëł27ÔÇ»%, China Ôëł4ÔÇ»%  
159	- 2030: SK hynix Ôëł40ÔÇ»%, Samsung Ôëł28.5ÔÇ»%, Micron Ôëł26.5ÔÇ»%, China Ôëł5ÔÇ»%  
160	
161	How this was constructed:
162	
163	- 2025ÔÇô2026 share anchors come from published HBM share data and a Deutsche Bank forecast: SK hynix 62ÔÇ»% of HBM in Q2ÔÇĹ25, Micron overtaking Samsung; 2026 HBM forecast: SK hynix 51ÔÇ»%, Samsung 25ÔÇ»%, Micron 24ÔÇ»%.ţłÇciteţłéturn2search1ţłéturn1search3ţłéturn1search9ţłéturn0search12ţłü  
164	- From 2027 onward, I slowly converge toward a somewhat more balanced market while keeping SK hynix as leader, reflecting MicronÔÇÖs more competitive roadmap and SamsungÔÇÖs catchÔÇĹup, but no data today suggests SK hynixÔÇÖs dominance collapses.ţłÇciteţłéturn1search1ţłéturn2news41ţłéturn6news40ţłéturn6news41ţłü  
165	- Chinese vendors remain constrained to lowÔÇĹsingleÔÇĹdigit global share through 2030 due to sanctions, lagging process technology, and a mainly domestic customer base.ţłÇciteţłéturn3search0ţłéturn3search6ţłéturn3search7ţłéturn3search4ţłéturn8news40ţłü  
166	
167	---
168	
169	### 2.3 Revenue contribution relative to DRAM businesses
170	
171	- Yole projects HBMÔÇÖs share of DRAM revenue rising from ~18ÔÇ»% in 2024 to >50ÔÇ»% by 2030.ţłÇciteţłéturn4search1ţłéturn8search5ţłéturn4search3ţłü  
172	- SK hynix has already disclosed that HBM accounts for roughly 40ÔÇ»% of its DRAM revenue as of late 2024/early 2025, and plans to double HBM sales again, implying HBM4 will be the *single largest* product line in its DRAM portfolio by the late 2020s.ţłÇciteţłéturn2news40ţłéturn2news41ţłéturn8search12ţłü  
173	- Under the highÔÇĹgrowth scenario above, by 2030 HBM4 alone could represent on the order of:
174	  - SK hynix: ÔëłUS$35ÔÇô40ÔÇ»B/year  
175	  - Samsung: ÔëłUS$25ÔÇô27ÔÇ»B/year  
176	  - Micron: ÔëłUS$25ÔÇ»B/year  
177	  which would make HBM4 the dominant profit engine for all three memory vendors.
178	
179	---
180	
181	### How to interpret this
182	
183	- The *direction* is robust:  
184	  - HBM4/HBM4E becomes the dominant HBM generation by 2027ÔÇô2028.  
185	  - HBM itself grows from a niche to a very large part of the DRAM market (approaching ~50ÔÇ»% of DRAM revenue by 2030 in aggressive scenarios).  
186	  - SK hynix stays in the lead, but Samsung and Micron both gain share vs their HBM3E positions.  
187	  - Chinese vendors grow but remain small globally through 2030.
188	
189	- The *absolute dollar values* are highly uncertain; published 2030 HBM revenue forecasts span roughly an order of magnitude. If you want a conservative range, scale all absolute numbers above down by a factor of ~3ÔÇô5 while keeping the share structure.
190

9	# HBM4 Breakthroughs and Yields
10	
11	HBM4 is only just moving from samples into early production, so public dataÔÇöespecially on yieldsÔÇöis sparse and often indirect. Below is a synthesis of what is reasonably grounded in public sources plus an explicit, modelled revenue / share scenario.
12	
13	---
14	
15	## 1. HBM4 status and yields by vendor (late 2025)
16	
17	### Technology baseline
18	
19	- JEDECÔÇÖs HBM4 standard (JESD238) was finalized in April 2025. It targets up to ~2ÔÇ»TB/s per stack, higher capacities (up to 64ÔÇ»GB/stack) and major powerÔÇĹefficiency gains vs HBM3E.ţłÇciteţłéturn0search9ţłéturn0search13ţłü  
20	- All three major suppliers (SK hynix, Samsung, Micron) are converging on 24ÔÇ»Gb DRAM dies, 12ÔÇĹHi and 16ÔÇĹHi stacks, and custom logic/base dies on advanced foundry nodes (TSMC 12FFC+/N5, Samsung 4ÔÇ»nm).ţłÇciteţłéturn8search2ţłéturn1search1ţłü  
21	- Industry roadmaps place HBM4 mass production in 2026, with HBM4EÔÇĹclass parts becoming the dominant HBM generation around 2027ÔÇô2028.ţłÇciteţłéturn5view0ţłéturn3news42ţłü  
22	
23	#### A note on ÔÇťyieldsÔÇŁ
24	
25	Reported ÔÇťyieldsÔÇŁ can mean:
26	
27	- FrontÔÇĹend DRAM die yield (per wafer)  
28	- Logic/baseÔÇĹdie yield  
29	- Final stacked HBM yield (all dies + TSV + packaging)
30	
31	Stack yields are much lower than individual die yields; for example, one analysis shows that 95ÔÇ»% perÔÇĹdie yield can still drop to ~44ÔÇ»% final yield on 16ÔÇĹHi stacks.ţłÇciteţłéturn6search6ţłü
32	
33	---
34	
35	### SK hynix
36	
37	Position: Clear HBM leader, already into HBM4 production ramp.
38	
39	Key HBM4 breakthroughs
40	
41	- Announced completion of HBM4 development in 2025; specs include up to 10ÔÇ»Gb/s per pin and Ôëł40ÔÇ»% better power efficiency than HBM3E.ţłÇciteţłéturn3search10ţłéturn1search3ţłü  
42	- Shipped HBM4 samples from March 2025, completed internal certification, and is preparing full mass production; major supplier to NVIDIA.ţłÇciteţłéturn3news42ţłéturn1news41ţłü  
43	- Roadmap (2026ÔÇĹ2028) adds 16ÔÇĹHi HBM4 and multiple HBM4E variants (8/12/16ÔÇĹHi), including custom baseÔÇĹdie designs that integrate the memory controller to free GPU area and cut interface power.ţłÇciteţłéturn8search14ţłü  
44	
45	Yields (publicly visible signals)
46	
47	- For HBM3E, SK hynix has reported production yields around 80ÔÇ»%, enabled by its MRÔÇĹMUF (Mass Reflow Molded Underfill) stacking process, explicitly framed as paving the way for HBM4 scalability.ţłÇciteţłéturn2search14ţłü  
48	- SK hynixÔÇÖs 1cÔÇĹnode DRAM (for future HBM4E) has been reported with test yields averaging >80ÔÇ»% and up to 90ÔÇ»% on some lots; HBM4 itself initially uses the more mature 1b node.ţłÇciteţłéturn2search3ţłéturn2search8ţłü  
49	- Analysts and press commentary consistently state that SK hynix enjoys the best effective HBM yields among the big three (HBM is ÔÇťmarginÔÇĹaccretiveÔÇŁ for SK hynix), while SamsungÔÇÖs are notably worse.ţłÇciteţłéturn6search14ţłéturn2search8ţłü  
50	
51	TakeÔÇĹaway: SK hynix enters HBM4 with mature packaging, strong HBM3E yields, and firstÔÇĹtoÔÇĹmarket status. It is widely expected to retain the largest HBM4 share through at least 2027ÔÇô2028.ţłÇciteţłéturn3news42ţłéturn2news41ţłü  
52	
53	---
54	
55	### Samsung
56	
57	Position: Very large DRAM maker, but playing catchÔÇĹup in HBM yields and NVIDIA qualification.
58	
59	Key HBM4 breakthroughs
60	
61	- Samsung is delivering HBM4 samples to NVIDIA (Nov 2025) and aims for earlyÔÇĹ2026 validation; volume production is targeted to align with nextÔÇĹgen GPUs.ţłÇciteţłéturn1search4ţłéturn2search4ţłéturn1search0ţłü  
62	- Uses 1cÔÇĹnode DRAM and a 4ÔÇ»nm logic/base die for HBM4, enabling tight integration and leveraging its foundry for custom logic dies.ţłÇciteţłéturn2search13ţłéturn2search5ţłéturn8search2ţłü  
63	- Reported to have significantly improved its AIÔÇĹmemory competitiveness in 2025, with aggressive expansion plans for HBM3E and HBM4 capacity as AI demand continues to exceed supply.ţłÇciteţłéturn6news41ţłéturn1search2ţłü  
64	
65	Yields
66	
67	- 1c DRAM used for HBM4 reportedly has sample yields around 50ÔÇ»% for HBM4 dies, with DDR5 yields above 70ÔÇ»%. Industry sources say Samsung wants Ôëł70ÔÇ»% HBM4 wafer yield before full mass production.ţłÇciteţłéturn2search5ţłéturn2search9ţłéturn2search3ţłü  
68	- SamsungÔÇÖs 4ÔÇ»nm HBM4 logic die reportedly exceeds 90ÔÇ»% yield, indicating the frontÔÇĹend logic is not the bottleneck.ţłÇciteţłéturn2search13ţłü  
69	- Earlier, SamsungÔÇÖs HBM3/HBM3E struggled to pass NVIDIAÔÇÖs tests due to heat and power issues, highlighting that its effective stack yields and systemÔÇĹlevel performance lagged SK hynix through 2024; that gap appears to be narrowing in late 2025.ţłÇciteţłéturn2search0ţłéturn2search4ţłéturn1search9ţłü  
70	
71	TakeÔÇĹaway: SamsungÔÇÖs HBM4 technology is competitive on paper, but effective yields and customer qualifications have trailed SK hynix. The trajectory in 2025 suggests improving yields, with room for share gains once HBM4 is in full volume.
72	
73	---
74	
75	### Micron
76	
77	Position: Late to HBM3, now very aggressive on HBM3E and HBM4; strong with NVIDIA on HBM3E and positioning HBM4E for 2027.
78	
79	Key HBM4 breakthroughs
80	
81	- Micron announced shipment of HBM4 samples in midÔÇĹ2025: 2048ÔÇĹbit interface, >2.0ÔÇ»TB/s bandwidth per stack, >60ÔÇ»% better performance vs its prior HBM, targeting nextÔÇĹgen AI platforms.ţłÇciteţłéturn1search5ţłéturn6news40ţłü  
82	- FollowÔÇĹup disclosures and roadmaps show pin speeds above 11ÔÇ»Gb/s and bandwidth up to Ôëł2.8ÔÇ»TB/s per stack, exceeding the JEDEC baseline.ţłÇciteţłéturn1search1ţłéturn6search1ţłéturn6search5ţłü  
83	- MicronÔÇÖs HBM4E (2027) will use custom baseÔÇĹlogic dies fabricated by TSMC, enabling customerÔÇĹspecific variants with higher gross margins.ţłÇciteţłéturn5view0ţłéturn6search5ţłéturn6search8ţłü  
84	
85	Yields
86	
87	- Multiple analyses (SemiAnalysis, investor notes) characterize MicronÔÇÖs HBM3E yields as competitive with or better than SK hynix and materially ahead of Samsung, enough that HBM is ÔÇťmarginÔÇĹaccretiveÔÇŁ despite lower absolute yields than commodity DRAM.ţłÇciteţłéturn6search14ţłéturn6search3ţłü  
88	- There are reports that Micron has had to redesign parts of its HBM4 due to yield/performance issues, potentially pushing some volume out toward 2027; this is framed as a temporary setback rather than a structural disadvantage.ţłÇciteţłéturn6search0ţłéturn6search13ţłü  
89	- No hard HBM4 yield numbers have been disclosed; based on HBM3E experience, it is reasonable to assume Micron aims for effective stack yields in the 50ÔÇô70ÔÇ»% range once HBM4 is fully ramped.
90	
91	TakeÔÇĹaway: Micron appears technologically competitive (fastest advertised HBM4 bandwidth today), but its HBM4 ramp is likely a bit later than SK hynix and Samsung. Its share gains are more visible in HBM3E, with HBM4/4E becoming a larger revenue driver from 2027 onward.
92	
93	---
94	
95	### Chinese vendors (CXMT, YMTC, Huawei ecosystem)
96	
97	There are no public indications that any Chinese vendor will massÔÇĹproduce JEDECÔÇĹcompliant HBM4 in the 2026ÔÇô2027 timeframe. Current efforts are focused on HBM3/HBM3EÔÇĹequivalent tech and inÔÇĹhouse/custom HBM for domestic AI accelerators.
98	
99	Key points
100	
101	- CXMT plans mass production of HBM3 around 2026 using MRÔÇĹMUF packaging, explicitly following SK hynixÔÇÖs process, and is targeting HBM3E around 2027; it remains ~3ÔÇô4 years behind the leaders.ţłÇciteţłéturn3search0ţłéturn3search6ţłéturn3search7ţłü  
102	- YMTC (primarily a NAND maker) is entering DRAM and HBM, developing TSVÔÇĹbased stacking and exploring partnership with CXMT for HBM.ţłÇciteţłéturn3search3ţłéturn3search4ţłéturn0search15ţłü  
103	- Huawei has announced its own HiBL 1.0 HBM for the AscendÔÇ»950PR (2026), claiming 128ÔÇ»GB and up to 1.6ÔÇ»TB/s bandwidth and saying it is more costÔÇĹeffective than industry HBM3E/HBM4E; a secondÔÇĹgen ÔÇťHiZQÔÇ»2.0ÔÇŁ is planned with 144ÔÇ»GB and up to 4ÔÇ»TB/s.ţłÇciteţłéturn3search2ţłéturn3search5ţłéturn3search8ţłéturn8search3ţłéturn8search15ţłü  
104	- Export controls on advanced tools and packaging are visibly constraining YMTC and other Chinese memory vendors; yields on leadingÔÇĹedge products made with domestic tools are widely reported to be lower than international peers.ţłÇciteţłéturn8news40ţłéturn3search4ţłü  
105	
106	TakeÔÇĹaway: Chinese players are unlikely to have material global HBM4 share before ~2028+, though they may play a growing role in the domestic China AI stack (Huawei Ascend, etc.). Their first volume products are more realistically HBM3/HBM3EÔÇĹclass.
107	
108	---
109	
110	## 2. Expected revenue contribution and market share for HBM4
111	
112	Because HBM4 is just ramping, any forward view has to be scenarioÔÇĹbased. The charts I generated above use a scenario primarily consistent with Yole GroupÔÇÖs aggressive HBM forecasts and TrendForceÔÇÖs HBM4E adoption timeline.
113	
114	### 2.1 Global HBM vs HBM4 revenue ÔÇô scenario
115	
116	Several recent forecasts:
117	
118	- Some market studies put HBM revenue at only ~US$2.5ÔÇô3ÔÇ»B in 2024, reaching ~US$5ÔÇô6ÔÇ»B by 2030 (very conservative).ţłÇciteţłéturn0search2ţłéturn4search11ţłéturn0search14ţłü  
119	- YoleÔÇĹbased work cited by multiple outlets instead projects HBM revenue growing from about US$17ÔÇ»B in 2024 to roughly US$98ÔÇ»B by 2030, a ~33ÔÇ»% CAGR.ţłÇciteţłéturn4search1ţłéturn4search3ţłéturn4search10ţłéturn4search12ţłü  
120

122	
123	| Year | Total HBM revenue (B$) | HBM4 family share of HBM | HBM4 revenue (B$) |
124	|------|------------------------|--------------------------|-------------------|

134	
135	- HBM4 (including HBM4E) begins meaningful volume in 2026, consistent with SK hynix and Samsung roadmaps.ţłÇciteţłéturn3news42ţłéturn1news41ţłéturn5view0ţłü  
136	- TrendForceÔÇÖs estimate that HBM4E alone is ~40ÔÇ»% of HBM demand by 2027 implies that HBM4 + HBM4E together dominate (>60ÔÇ»%) from 2027 onward.ţłÇciteţłéturn5view0ţłü  
137	
138	If instead you adopt the more conservative ÔÇťHBM ~US$6ÔÇ»B in 2030ÔÇŁ forecasts, you can roughly divide all HBM4 revenue numbers above by ~15ÔÇô16; the vendor *shares* below would be similar, but absolute dollars far smaller.

143

146	1. HBM4 revenue by vendor (2026ÔÇô2030)  
147	2. HBM4 market share by vendor (2026ÔÇô2030)

159	Implied HBM4 marketÔÇĹshare paths:

161	- 2026: SK hynix Ôëł50ÔÇ»%, Samsung Ôëł25ÔÇ»%, Micron Ôëł24ÔÇ»%, China Ôëł1ÔÇ»%  
162	- 2027: SK hynix Ôëł49ÔÇ»%, Samsung Ôëł24.5ÔÇ»%, Micron Ôëł24.5ÔÇ»%, China Ôëł2ÔÇ»%

176	
177	- Yole projects HBMÔÇÖs share of DRAM revenue rising from ~18ÔÇ»% in 2024 to >50ÔÇ»% by 2030.ţłÇciteţłéturn4search1ţłéturn8search5ţłéturn4search3ţłü
